speaker: interviewer
text: March 18, 1997. Interview with Ester Gever, born Schachter. My name is Lilian Gewirtzman, Rego Park, New York, USA. The language is English. March 18, 1997, interview with Ester Gever, born Schachter. My name is Lilian Gewirtzman, G-E-W-I-R-T-Z-M-A-N, Rego Park, New York, USA. Language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Mrs. Gever. Would you please tell us your full name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning. My name is Esther Gever, E-S-T-H-E-R, Gever, G-E-V-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the town and country you were born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm born in Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date is April 14, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old does that make you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next month I'll be 67.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel Moses Schachter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a shoe designer. He was a little bit of a musician, but his living he made from designing shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Otylia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mandel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was also born in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have any occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She was a housewife.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were two sisters. My sister is born during the-- a month after the Anschluss. I was eight years old at the time. And was an only child for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's name is Ruth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her present last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schachter. She is presently single.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any extended family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of my family perished during the war. Some of them in Siberia, and most of them in Germany, Austria, Yugoslavia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell me about your extended family-- grandparents, uncles--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --aunts, cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had my mother's side family lived in Vienna. She had one sister and three children. She had two brothers-- one brother had two sons, and one brother had two sons and two daughters. My grandmother lived in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Bella Mandel. And she was 70-- in her 70s-- I think 75-- when I last saw her, when we parted. And it was a very difficult time for me because I was very attached to her. She was the most wonderful thing in my life, throughout my life, my memories with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far back do your memories go?

speaker: interviewee
text: My memories go very far back. I remember her babysitting for me because my parents loved ballroom dancing. And I spent a lot of time-- she had 14 grandchildren, I was the youngest, and she made me feel her favorite. She had daily tickets for the theater, the Yiddish theater in Vienna. And 99% of the time I was to accompany her. She saw me on a daily basis. She used to say such wonderful things to me. We-- I could never do anything wrong when she was around. No one would hit me or touch me or scream at me. I was always protected.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she say to you? What are the wonderful things?

speaker: interviewee
text: She used to say, 'When I look at you, [GERMAN].' That was such a silly thing, but everything from the past is so dear. And even silly little things mean so much because it was a beautiful life, it was wiped out, it never happen again, it will never--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She was very independent. She lived alone. She visited most of her children on a daily basis. She was very energetic. To get around in Vienna, it was not next door, but she made sure that all her children are OK. And she was sort of a Robin Hood, because if some of her children had more money than the others, she'd try to equalize it. She tried to touch their conscience that their brother needs help or something. That I remember. And she always told me that wealth means nothing. If you can't share it, if you see someone suffer, then you really cannot be happy with your money or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you call her? [NON-ENGLISH], grandmama. Was there a grandpa?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My grandfather I never met because he died when my mother was seven months old. And that's how they got to Vienna, because my mother was born in Lemberg, Lwow, in Poland. My grandmother had seven children. She-- after her husband passed away, she packed up, came to Vienna, and had all her children educated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why to Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was easier for her to provide for her children. And she organized women and she was making shirts, sewing shirts, men's shirts, and selling them to stores, and she educated all her children. They were all well-off, most of them. And very independent, very enterprising, and very loving, very warm. A real mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have parents too?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's parents lived in Poland, and unfortunately, I met them only after we left Vienna. They were wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any other family members live with you or in close proximity?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's sister lived in walking distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clara Fleisig. She had three sons and one daughter. And there were family weddings and I used to be the flower girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close to any of your cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, especially to one. One of my-- Uncle Josef's youngest daughter, she was-- she belonged to a Zionist organizations, and they were-- her parents forbid her to do that. She was 15, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Marvina, and in order to attend meetings she needed someone to cover for her. And she used to take me supposedly for a walk in the park, and we went to those meetings. And I felt very grown up that she gave me that kind of responsibility. I never told anyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was six. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then there were my grandmother's birthday parties every year. The whole family gathered, and she loved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- she loved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you gather?

speaker: interviewee
text: In her home. They all came to her. She used to make cakes and there were elaborate dinners. And what I remember, she used to make eggnog. She loved eggnog. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents. My mother took care of my behavior. She liked me to be very prim and proper. My father was a balance, because he was sneering at conventional things. He was kind of a rebel. He was-- We had discussions-- we-- we did not-- I did not, but I was present at the discussions at the table. There were always discussions about convention, and about music, and about theater, and about family, and it was never a dull moment. My parents were very communicative.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she want you to be proper?

speaker: interviewee
text: How. I was-- First of all, eating habits were very important to her, and I thank her for it. Greeting, how to greet. Kiss of the hand was the proper way at the time. You-- Not to speak unless asked. When grownups speak you don't interrupt. The politeness. The-- the-- the aesthetics of behavior is what she taught me. And till this day when people are contrary to that, it bothers me. I tried to do the same job on my children, but it's-- it's the 20th century and it progressed. Behavior is not always proper.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of religious holidays did your family observe?

speaker: interviewee
text: All holidays. We were modern religious. My father went to temple every Saturday, and I accompanied him many, many times. All holidays were observed. We had a kosher home. But my father thought nothing of it to-- like to take-- put the switch on to turn the light on. He felt that it's absolutely ridiculous not to do that. That's not work. And that's how I learned to question everything in life. I did like the tradition, and I still do, but my father's way. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the family friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Family friends. I don't have anyone from the past. I don't-- We lost contact with anyone from Austria because we escaped Poland. And few Austrian Jews went that route. I remember my father's friends, some of them. I can't remember their names. I don't. There were coffee klatches, there were neighbors, very nice neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends were not all Jewish?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father's?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were. I think they were. Although, he did not believe that the Jews are-- have the exclusivity on humanity. I mean he convinced himself otherwise later. But at the time, he was really rebellious and felt that all people are wonderful and nice, if you give them a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your friends? Who were your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends were-- I went to public school and I had friends in my building that were Christian girls that came to my house on our holidays, and loved all the goodies and sweet things, and we played together with my toys. And it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel any different from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I really did not. I was taught to love everyone, unless someone deserves not to be loved. And I loved my friends, and we loved our neighbors. It's a matter of fact, I remember we had one neighbor that was gentile-- they came from Czechoslovakia to Vienna. I was in their house on a daily basis. If I didn't come to visit them-- they were childless. If I didn't come to visit them, they would come and look for me. And we played Dominoes. When they had a Christmas tree, they allowed me to pick of their tree, the sweets. I enjoyed it very much. And they were true friends to us till the end. They risked their lives to bring us food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we have their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frau Coltzel and Herr Coltzel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your home look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had beautiful things. We had paintings. I remember our bedroom set particularly was so gorgeous. It was called [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] wood. And the living room, there-- there were carpets, there were lamps and crystals. And my parents loved antiques. They used to buy antiques. They-- They loved rustic kind of look. And I inherited a lot of that. I look for the same things when I buy. It makes me feel warm and comfortable, and it reminds me of my past. And they did not really put that much emphasis-- it was not-- money and things never meant that much to my parents. It was human touch between friends, politics, books, music.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the neighborhood like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in [INAUDIBLE], which is-- was mostly Jewish. It was like, I guess, the east side of New York. It was very much. There were a lot of temples. We belonged-- to the temple we were going was a very large temple, but I cannot remember the name. It was a big temple, a beautiful temple. And there was a balcony, and when I went with my mother I was in the balcony. When I went with my father, I stayed with him downstairs. I was born in [INAUDIBLE], which is I think different. It was not as Jewish. But when I was six years old, I had scarlet fever and I had to be hospitalized. At that time it was a very infectious and dangerous disease. And when my parents came to pick me up from the hospital after several weeks, they told me we have a surprise for you. And that's when they took me to our new apartment, and they gave me a beautiful room, beautifully furnished.

speaker: interviewer
text: What color?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a white metal bed, white. Very lacey, very frilly, very girlish. I had a wicker chair and a beautiful stove, a wood stove, that was installed for the winter and removed during the summer. And during the winter when the wind was howling I used to sit in that chair and wait for my father, because when my father wasn't there, it was-- I was insecure about the weather and the thunder and everything else. So I used to sit in that chair, look into the fire and wait, and whenever I heard his footsteps I used to rise up and run [SOBBING] and meet him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I went to school. I had a wonderful teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a school was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a public school. My teacher's name was Frau Zauer. She was wonderful. Going even further back, I remember being in kindergarten. I remember performing in a play.

speaker: interviewer
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember what it was, but there were girls, and I remember a sentence, and I said, '[GERMAN].' I was serving coffee to someone, and I don't remember what the play was about. But I remember kindergarten. So I started school at six-- I must have been five years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the school experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. I learned to read very fast I remember. I don't think math was exactly my subject. When there was math, I used to get a little ill. I think my stomach hurt or something. But reading and painting and the writing, I loved very much. It was short-- only three years. And actually, the third year was interrupted with the Anschluss.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the Anschluss, did you feel any difference between the Jewish and non-Jewish children in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I did not. Absolutely not.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the teachers treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. Very well. I was a very happy child-- an extremely happy child. I had everything. I had a loving family. I had everything I ever wanted. I had a father who was educating me and taking me through Vienna and showing me everything, making me aware-- architecture, museums, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you used to listen to what adults talked about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear them speak about Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Radio. They were sitting at the radio. And I sensed they did not want to make me feel frightened. But I sensed it. I don't remember what it was. I cannot recall what they-- I know-- I just know that there was impending danger. And being that I only experienced good things, danger was so far. It was such an unfamiliar emotion.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you sense that?

speaker: interviewee
text: By watching my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: By watching friends. Nervousness, fear, insecurity. Saying is this going to happen? Maybe it won't happen. Maybe the world will not allow it. I do remember certain sentences. And I always asked my father about things that I didn't understand. But he would never talk to me about this. He would never talk to me about it. I used to interview my father for hours. If I didn't feel like sleeping, and instead of bed time stories which I knew already by heart, I used to ask him how do you make iron, or how you make glass, or where one thing or another comes from. And he used to have all the answers. Of course, my father was the smartest man on earth. I used to be very proud of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you also ask him what they were afraid of?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. He said, 'No,' he says, 'It's politics.' 'It's politics. Don't worry about it. Everything will be OK.'

speaker: interviewer
text: You keep speaking of the Anschluss. Do you remember the Anschluss?

speaker: interviewee
text: I certainly do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember going to sleep, and in the morning the-- they communicated we are now part of Germany. And stores, Jewish stores, right away looted, broken windows, and guess what? My friends didn't like me anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: My gentile friends that I was very close and I loved, and I wanted to play with them and they didn't want me. [SOBBING] I didn't understand it. That was the first taste of discrimination. And from there on it became worse. I used to walk in the streets and if we saw a SS man or any uniformed German, you were supposed to say heil Hitler. And I was afraid not to say it, that they will harm me. [SOBBING] And I used to say it and feel unclean by doing that. It was-- I was frightened because every night in my sleep I was awakened by terrorizing Jews in the streets, men crying out from pain. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was terror. It turned overnight into a nightmare.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see personally about the men crying out in pain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men with bearded men were beaten. [SOBBING] Sometimes there'd be torn beards to the blood. They made to wash-- painted the sidewalks, made them-- knee-- old men kneel and to wash sidewalks. They used to demean-- if they saw a woman that was well dressed and attractive-- We had a neighbor, a very attractive woman, her little boy was a friend of mine. And one day they came--

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop now.
